peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness to experience and strong GAL-TAN pluralism and participatory democracy preference drive support, though low conscientiousness tempers absolute enthusiasm.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (83.73) and populist people-centrism (76.71) drive moderate support, but low openness (30.3) and high conscientiousness (99.56) temper enthusiasm for untested deliberative formats.

High openness, very low agreeableness, and strong GAL-TAN green/alternative/libertarian participatory democracy preference combine with ambivalent trust in formal institutions to produce mild support for an innovative, inclusive but potentially inefficient democratic mechanism.
High openness and populist people-centrism support participatory democracy, but low agreeableness and distrust in institutions temper enthusiasm.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (83.33) and libertarian anti-domination (76.29) drive moderate support, but very low openness (13.56) and low conscientiousness (11.23) temper enthusiasm for unstructured deliberative processes.

Very low openness to experience and high authoritarianism make participatory democracy feel inefficient and destabilizing, but high agreeableness and populist anti-elitism yield moderate rather than maximal opposition.

High openness and libertarian civil liberties support favor participatory democracy, but low GAL-TAN green-alternative-libertarian scores and high system justification temper enthusiasm for replacing representative structures.

High populist people-centrism (94.75) and anti-elitism (89.5) drive support for citizen-led deliberation, but low openness (20.68) and very high agreeableness (84.7) temper enthusiasm for unstructured participatory experiments.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support arises from very high agreeableness and fairness values, but weak external political efficacy and a competitive worldview create ambivalence.

Strong respect for rules (justice value) and high dutifulness (99.78) support procedural acceptance, yet high collective narcissism (94.21) and a dangerous worldview (79.16) create some reluctance to concede to out-groups.

Strong authoritarian submission (very low) and social dominance orientation (low) are countered by high competitive jungle worldview and moderate ethnic nationalism, leading to mild ambivalence about conceding to political opponents.
Strong support for democratic norms and high political trust align with endorsing loser consent, though low agreeableness may reduce full commitment.

Strong civic nationalism (75.78) and high agreeableness (95.64) support fair-play norms, while very low system justification (5.37) and low RWA (19.7) reduce deference to winners, yielding clear but not maximal endorsement.
High agreeableness and strong preference for social harmony are offset by very low political trust and a fearful-avoidant attachment style that suspects the system will not reciprocate fairness.
High agreeableness, dutifulness, and system justification support accepting electoral outcomes, though a history of legal system contact and moderate external efficacy introduce slight skepticism.

Very high trust in electoral process (99.24) and strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation (72.37) support loser consent, yet high social dominance orientation (89.57) introduces a slight reservation about fully empowering out-groups.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high agreeableness and deference to legitimate authority provide a solid base, though authoritarian punitiveness and a dangerous-worldview add reservations about disruption.

Very high conscientiousness (99.56) and high trust in government (79.86) align with strong support for orderly transitions, though a history of coercive control exposure adds a slight wariness about institutional reliability.

High civic nationalism, very low RWA, and moderate trust in political institutions support a clear but not strong endorsement of the norm, while past exposure to political violence slightly tempers enthusiasm.
High authoritarian law-and-order preference and institutional trust strongly support peaceful transfers, despite low agreeableness.

Very high agreeableness (95.64) and strong anti-violence values (rejection of political violence implied by low authoritarian aggression at 5.13) combine with high trust (98.8) to produce strong support for this core democratic norm.

High authoritarian law-and-order orientation and the value of predictability strongly support peaceful transitions, but low system justification and past criminal-justice experience temper full endorsement.
Very high conscientiousness, high dutifulness, and strong conventionalism from RWA strongly endorse orderly transitions, while low dangerous-worldview reduces fear-driven exceptions.

Extremely low traditional-authoritarian-nationalist values (1.93) and high anti-authoritarianism (5.14 RWA) strongly endorse peaceful transfers, though a criminal record for protest-related offenses adds a nuanced personal stake in orderly transitions.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarian skepticism of state control and anti-censorship orientation clash with the idea of mandated participation, despite moderate communitarian values.

High libertarian civil liberties support (31.28) and anti-domination moral foundation (88.7) create mild opposition to mandated participation, but high dutifulness and authoritarian law-and-order preference (57.56) pull toward neutrality.

Very high libertarianism (anti-state control, civil liberties) and high internal political efficacy make compulsory voting feel coercive, though high civic engagement and moderate trust in the system prevent maximal opposition.
Libertarian anti-state control and low civic engagement lead to opposition, but high conscientiousness and authoritarian submission create mild ambivalence.

Extreme libertarian anti-state-control (99.4) and high civil liberties support (90.49) generate opposition to mandatory participation, despite high voting participation tendency (66.31) and moderate civic engagement.

Authoritarian submission is high but libertarianism scores are very low and political participation is erratic, creating a conflicted stance where coercion feels wrong yet voting seems marginally desirable.
Very high libertarianism and anti-authoritarianism scores oppose mandatory participation, but high system justification and external efficacy create mild ambivalence about its stabilizing effects.

Very high libertarian anti-state-control (95.65) and free-spirit autonomy (72.68) oppose compulsory voting, but high civic volunteering (96.23) and voting participation (86.27) create mild ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong GAL-TAN orientation and populist people-centrism favor checks on power, but low system justification and trust in government limit enthusiasm.

High respect for rules and order (conscientiousness order 90.48) supports constitutional limits, yet high populist anti-elitism (91.68) and popular sovereignty (87.34) generate skepticism about elite-driven constraints.

High openness to values and low authoritarian submission support constitutional limits, but moderate system justification and trust in existing institutions reduce the sense of urgency, yielding mild support.
High respect for authority and tradition, combined with populist anti-elitism, produce moderate support for constitutional limits on power.

High authoritarianism law-and-order preference (97.04) and strong leader preference (82.98) create ambivalence toward constraints on power, but high GAL-TAN civil liberties (87.46) and low system justification (5.37) push toward moderate support.
Populist people-centrism and anti-elitism distrust fixed constitutional constraints imposed by elites, yet high conscientiousness and need for predictability provide slight support for stable rules.
High RWA conventionalism and system justification favor rule-of-law constraints, while high openness and disruptor tendencies push against rigid limits on change.

Strong populist popular sovereignty (86.09) and anti-elitism (89.5) support constitutional limits on power, while high system justification (57.55) and trust in government (95.19) ensure endorsement is moderate rather than maximal.

High local community participation, low trust in national institutions relative to local, and high openness to new structures favor decentralization, though moderate nationalism tempers enthusiasm.
Strong national unity preference and authoritarianism favor centralization, but high openness and libertarianism create mild counter-pressure.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Openness to new systems, populist people-centrism, and limited trust in current legislatures favor proportional representation.
High GAL-TAN pluralism (94.8) and populist people-centrism (76.71) favor proportional representation, but high conscientiousness and need for order (90.48) prefer stable majoritarian systems.
High openness to new systems, low majoritarian bias, and participatory preference favor proportional representation, while moderate nationalism and moderate system justification slow enthusiasm.
High openness and populist people-centrism support proportionality, but authoritarian preference for strong leadership and low GAL orientation moderate support.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy (83.33) and popular sovereignty (68.62) favor proportional representation, but very low openness (13.56) and high ethnic nationalism (90.65) prefer familiar majoritarian systems, resulting in mild support.
Low openness and traditionalism make major electoral system changes unsettling, and competitive-jungle worldview views proportional representation as weakening decisive leadership.
High openness and low GAL-TAN green-alternative-libertarian scores produce mild support for reform, but high system justification and trust in current electoral process limit enthusiasm.

High populist people-centrism (94.75) and low traditional-authoritarian-nationalist values (1.93) support proportional representation, but low openness (20.68) and high system justification (57.55) create moderate rather than strong enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Respect for rules and legitimate authority support judicial independence, but personal mistrust of the judiciary and low system justification reduce enthusiasm.

High trust in judiciary (75.32), respect for rules, and high conscientiousness strongly support judicial independence, though populist anti-elitism (91.68) creates some wariness of unaccountable judges.

High trust in judiciary, very low authoritarian submission, and moderate external efficacy support protecting judicial independence, though past incarceration experience and low agreeableness-trust add caution.
High trust in judiciary and authoritarian respect for legitimate authority strongly support judicial independence, despite low agreeableness.

High GAL-TAN civil liberties (87.46) and low trust in judiciary (40.11) paradoxically support protecting independence, while high authoritarianism law-and-order (97.04) and strong leader preference (82.98) create some opposition, yielding clear moderate support.
Profound distrust in judiciary makes protecting its independence feel like shielding an untrusted institution from democratic accountability, despite high abstract support for rule of law.
High system justification, RWA conventionalism, and moderate trust-in-judiciary support independence, while personal legal system contact introduces slight skepticism about judicial impartiality.

Very high trust in judiciary (81.13) and low authoritarian submission (18.56) strongly support judicial independence, though high social dominance orientation (89.57) and collective narcissism (83.9) introduce a slight preference for judicial accountability to the nation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Near-neutral trust reflects moderate local resource access and community participation offset by overall low external political efficacy and populist skepticism.

High trust in government (79.86) and high conscientiousness support trust in civil service, but high anti-elitism (91.68) and low external efficacy (20.83) generate moderate skepticism about bureaucratic competence.
Moderate general institutional trust and high external efficacy are offset by low trust in straightforwardness, moderate populist anti-elitism, and lived bureaucratic friction as a refugee.
High institutional trust and authoritarian submission support trust in civil service, tempered by populist anti-elitism.

Low trust in government (43.91) and very low system justification (5.37) produce mild distrust, though high agreeableness trust (98.8) and personal experience with bureaucracy (disability benefits) create some countervailing trust.
Moderate neighborhood access and some positive civic engagement experience are outweighed by very low general institutional trust and a bureaucratic skepticism born from statelessness.

High external efficacy and system justification support trust, but low local service access, moderate bridging social capital, and personal experience with credential recognition issues reduce confidence.

High general trust in government (95.19) and trust in science/experts (97.02) support trust in civil service, but high populist anti-elitism (89.5) and low community participation (0) create mild skepticism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Mild distrust stems from very low political trust and system justification, despite occasional civic engagement and a preference for democracy.
High trust in government (79.86) and high dutifulness support trust, but high collective narcissism (94.21) and dangerous worldview (79.16) introduce concerns about electoral integrity and fairness.
High trust in electoral process (profile) is nearly canceled by very high anti-elitism, suspicion of institutions (low agreeableness-trust), and moderate ethnic nationalism, leaving near-neutrality.
High political trust and system justification, combined with authoritarian respect for order, produce strong trust in electoral process.

Moderate trust in institutions (44.84) and high voting participation (66.31) provide some trust, but very low system justification (5.37) and high anti-elitism (56.06) generate skepticism, resulting in mild trust.
Extremely low political trust and external efficacy, combined with past system contact and a competitive-jungle worldview, produce strong belief that electoral processes are rigged.
High system justification and external efficacy support trust, but low perceived safety, personal legal system contact, and moderate populist attitudes create measured skepticism.

Very high stated trust in electoral process (99.24) and high voting participation (86.27) support trust, but high populist anti-elitism (89.5) and a criminal record for protest-related offenses create moderate rather than strong trust.
